title: Introduction to Static Arrays and Memory Layout
content: A static array is a contiguous block of memory allocated to store elements of the same type. Because elements are stored side-by-side, we can calculate the memory address of any index in O(1) time using the formula: Address = BaseAddress + Index * ElementSize. Accessing an element at index 'i' takes O(1) time. However, inserting or deleting an element at an arbitrary index requires shifting elements, which takes O(N) time in the worst case. The capacity of a static array cannot be changed once it is allocated.

title: Deep Dive: Dynamic Arrays and Amortized Reallocation
content: Dynamic arrays (like ArrayList in Java or std::vector in C++) solve the fixed-size limitation of static arrays. When a dynamic array becomes full and you attempt to append a new element, the system automatically allocates a new block of memory (usually double the current capacity), copies all existing elements over to the new block, and then inserts the new element. Although the copy operation takes O(N) time, this capacity doubling happens infrequently. Mathematically, appending N elements takes O(N) total time, meaning the average (amortized) cost of a single append operation is O(1). However, the worst-case insertion time remains O(N) due to the possible trigger of a database copy/resize event.

title: Singly Linked Lists: Basics and Operations
content: A singly linked list is a linear data structure where elements are not stored in contiguous memory locations. Instead, each element (called a Node) contains two fields: 'data' (the value of the element) and 'next' (a pointer/reference to the next node in the list). The entry point of a linked list is the Head. Because nodes are linked via pointers, inserting or deleting a node at the beginning of the list requires only changing the head reference and the new node's next pointer, taking O(1) time. Unlike arrays, there is no random access; to access the i-th element, you must traverse from the head node one-by-one, which takes O(N) time.

title: Floyd's Cycle-Finding Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare)
content: Floyd's Cycle-Finding Algorithm is a pointer-based algorithm used to detect cycles in linked lists. It uses two pointers, which move through the sequence at different speeds. The slow pointer ('tortoise') moves forward one node at a time, while the fast pointer ('hare') moves forward two nodes at a time. If there is no cycle, the fast pointer will reach the end of the list (None) and terminate. If a cycle exists, the fast pointer will eventually wrap around and meet the slow pointer at the exact same node (slow == fast). This algorithm is highly efficient as it runs in O(N) time and requires only O(1) auxiliary space.

title: Stacks: Concept and Practical Implementations
content: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) protocol. Think of a stack of plates: the last plate placed on top is the first one to be removed. The two primary operations are: 'push' (adds an element to the top of the stack) and 'pop' (removes the element from the top of the stack). Both operations take O(1) time. Stacks are heavily used in function call management (the execution stack), recursion execution, undo/redo features in applications, and syntax parsing.

title: Operator Precedence and Infix to Postfix Conversion
content: To convert an mathematical infix expression (like A + B * C) to postfix notation (like A B C * +) using a stack: we scan the expression from left to right. Operands (like A, B, C) are appended directly to the output string. Operators (like +, *, -) are pushed onto a stack. Before pushing an operator, we must pop all operators from the stack that have equal or higher precedence than the incoming operator. Once the scan is complete, we pop any remaining operators off the stack and append them to the output. This ensures correct mathematical order of operations without using parentheses.

title: Queues: Concept and Queue Implementations
content: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the First-In, First-Out (FIFO) protocol. Elements are added at the back (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue). Both primary operations take O(1) time when implemented correctly. Queues are used for asynchronous data transfers, print queue managers, and scheduling algorithms.

title: Circular Queues and Capacity Optimization
content: In a linear array queue, dequeuing elements leaves empty slots at the beginning of the array which cannot be reused, leading to memory waste. A Circular Queue solves this by wrapping the end of the array back to the beginning. We manage two pointers: 'front' and 'rear'. The rear pointer wraps around using modulus arithmetic: rear = (rear + 1) % Capacity. The queue is full when the next position of the rear pointer meets the front pointer: (rear + 1) % Capacity == front. This allows O(1) operations with maximum memory utilization.

title: Binary Trees: Concept and Properties
content: A tree is a hierarchical data structure. A Binary Tree is a tree where each parent node can have at most two children, referred to as the left child and the right child. The top node is called the Root node. Nodes with no children are called Leaf nodes. The depth of a node is the length of the path from the root to the node. Binary trees are the foundation of Binary Search Trees, heaps, and syntax tree parsing.

title: Binary Search Trees (BST) and In-order Traversal
content: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree with a key property: for any node, all keys in its left subtree are smaller than the node's key, and all keys in its right subtree are larger than the node's key. This property makes searching, insertion, and deletion highly efficient (O(log N) average case). Furthermore, performing an In-Order traversal (Left subtree -> Root -> Right subtree) on a valid BST always retrieves the keys in fully sorted, ascending order. This sorted output is a direct mathematical consequence of the BST property.

title: Unbalanced BST and Worst-case Operations
content: While BSTs offer O(log N) search times when balanced, they can degrade to linear O(N) performance if elements are inserted in fully sorted or reverse-sorted order. In this scenario, the tree forms a single long chain resembling a linked list (skewed tree). To search, insert, or delete, you must traverse all N nodes. This is why self-balancing trees like AVL Trees or Red-Black Trees are used in database engines: they automatically restructure themselves during insertion/deletion to maintain O(log N) height.

title: Graph Representation and Breadth First Search (BFS)
content: A graph is a network of vertices connected by edges. Graphs can be represented using an Adjacency Matrix or Adjacency List. Breadth-First Search (BFS) is a graph traversal algorithm that starts at a source vertex and explores all neighboring nodes at the current depth before moving deeper. BFS uses a Queue to manage nodes to visit next and a boolean array to track visited nodes. Because BFS explores levels layer-by-layer, it guarantees finding the shortest path (in terms of minimum number of edges) in an unweighted graph.

title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm and Heaps
content: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative weights. It works by repeatedly selecting the unvisited vertex with the minimum distance and relaxing its adjacent edges. To find the minimum-distance vertex efficiently, we use a Min-Priority Queue (usually implemented via a Binary Min-Heap). With a binary heap, extracting the minimum distance takes O(log V) time, and relaxing an edge takes O(log V) time, leading to an overall runtime complexity of O((V + E) log V). Using a simple array instead of a heap would take O(V^2) time.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming (DP)
content: Dynamic Programming (DP) is an algorithm design technique used to solve complex optimization problems by breaking them down into smaller subproblems. To use DP, a problem must possess two key properties: (1) Overlapping Subproblems: the problem can be broken down into subproblems which are reused multiple times, and (2) Optimal Substructure: the optimal solution to the problem can be constructed from optimal solutions of its subproblems. We cache the results of subproblems in a table to avoid redundant computations, using Memoization (Top-down) or Tabulation (Bottom-up).

title: The 0/1 Knapsack Problem and Tabulation
content: The 0/1 Knapsack problem asks you to select a subset of items, each with a weight and a value, to pack in a knapsack of capacity W such that the total value is maximized and the total weight is <= W. The '0/1' constraint means each item is either taken or not. A brute-force search takes O(2^N) time. Using Dynamic Programming, we construct a 2D table of size (N+1) x (W+1) where table[i][w] represents the maximum value achievable with the first 'i' items and capacity 'w'. We compute values using the formula: table[i][w] = max(table[i-1][w], value[i] + table[i-1][w - weight[i]]) if weight[i] <= w. This tabulated approach reduces the time complexity to a highly efficient O(N * W).
